subject: EU Law
lesson_title: Effects of a Preliminary Ruling and Interim Relief

questions.question: A preliminary ruling on interpretation under Article 267 TFEU has what temporal effect?

options.A: It applies only from the date of the ruling — prospective effect only
options.B: It is retrospective — it clarifies what the law has always meant from the date of adoption of the EU act; it applies to legal relationships arising before the ruling (ex tunc effect), unless the CJEU limits its temporal effect
options.C: It applies only to the specific parties to the national proceedings and only for that case
options.D: It applies from the date the national court delivers its judgment based on the ruling

questions.explanation: A CJEU interpretation ruling is declaratory — it clarifies what EU law has always meant, not what it will mean in future. It therefore applies retrospectively (ex tunc) to all legal relationships, including those arising before the ruling was given. This means litigants in other pending cases can benefit from the ruling. However, the CJEU has the power (exercised rarely) to limit the temporal effect of its ruling — permitting parties in concluded cases to rely on the ruling only in limited circumstances — to protect legal certainty and avoid massive disruption to established arrangements.

questions.question: When may the CJEU limit the temporal effects of a preliminary ruling?

options.A: Whenever the ruling overturns established national case law
options.B: Where two conditions are cumulatively met: (1) there is a good faith objective uncertainty about the meaning of EU law (parties acted in good faith relying on an established interpretation); (2) serious economic consequences would flow from giving the ruling full retrospective effect
options.C: Whenever the Commission requests a prospective-only ruling
options.D: In all cases involving social security rights, to protect national budgets

questions.explanation: The CJEU limits temporal effect only where: (1) there was objective uncertainty — authorities/parties acted in good faith, reasonably believing their conduct was consistent with EU law; and (2) the retrospective application would cause serious economic disruption. Both conditions must be met. Limitation of temporal effects has been applied, for example, in Defrenne No.2 (equal pay) and Barber (pension rights). The CJEU limits this power narrowly — effective enforcement requires that rulings generally have full retrospective force.

questions.question: In Zuckerfabrik Süderdithmarschen [1991] (Joined Cases C-143/88 and C-92/89), the CJEU held that national courts have power to grant interim relief suspending application of a national measure implementing an EU Regulation pending a preliminary ruling. The conditions the CJEU established were:

options.A: The applicant must show serious economic hardship and the EU act must clearly violate a Treaty provision
options.B: The national court must have serious doubts about the validity of the EU act; urgency (serious irreparable harm would result from application); and the national court must take account of the Union's interests
options.C: The Commission must have initiated an investigation of the EU act's validity before national courts can grant interim suspension
options.D: Interim suspension is never permitted pending a validity reference — the EU act must remain in force until the CJEU rules

questions.explanation: Zuckerfabrik established that national courts may suspend application of a national measure implementing an EU act pending a validity reference if: (1) the court has serious doubts about the validity of the EU act (and has made or will make a reference); (2) urgency — it would suffer serious and irreparable harm if interim relief is refused; (3) the national court takes account of the Union's interests (the act is presumed valid until the CJEU rules; that presumption must be respected). These conditions balance effective judicial protection with the need not to undermine EU act validity unduly.

questions.question: In Atlanta Fruchthandelsgesellschaft [1995] (Case C-465/93), the CJEU extended Zuckerfabrik to allow national courts to grant positive interim measures (not just suspension) in what circumstances?

options.A: Whenever the applicant shows the EU act is unlawful
options.B: National courts may grant positive interim measures (e.g., requiring the administration to pay a sum or take action) pending a validity ruling, applying the same Zuckerfabrik conditions adapted to the context of positive relief
options.C: Only where the Commission has already brought an infringement action against the member state
options.D: National courts may never grant positive interim measures — only suspension is permitted

questions.explanation: Atlanta extended Zuckerfabrik to positive interim relief — not just suspension of the national implementing measure but positive orders requiring authorities to act (e.g., issue import licences pending a ruling on the validity of the EU quota regime). The same substantive conditions apply: serious doubts about validity, urgency/irreparable harm, and taking account of EU interests. The Court recognised that restricting interim relief to suspension would leave applicants without adequate protection where positive action was needed.

questions.question: Can national courts grant interim relief against application of a directly effective EU law provision itself (rather than a national implementing measure)?

options.A: Yes — national courts have broad power to grant any interim relief needed to protect EU law rights
options.B: Only in very limited circumstances and subject to the same Zuckerfabrik-type conditions applied to EU acts directly — national courts cannot simply disapply a directly effective EU law rule on an interim basis
options.C: No — national courts may never suspend application of directly effective EU law
options.D: Yes, but only with Commission approval

questions.explanation: Where the challenged measure is the EU act itself (not a national implementing act), national courts may in principle grant interim relief pending a CJEU ruling, but subject to the strict Zuckerfabrik conditions. The presumption of validity of EU acts means interim suspension of an EU act itself requires very serious doubts about validity and urgency. Directly effective EU law provisions cannot simply be set aside by a national court — that would undermine the coherence and supremacy of EU law. Any interim suspension of an EU act must be narrow, necessary, and consistent with the interests of the EU legal order.

questions.question: The principle from Factortame I [1990] (C-213/89) that is relevant to interim relief is:

options.A: That national courts must grant any interim relief sought by an EU law claimant
options.B: That if a national rule of procedural law prevents a national court from granting interim relief to protect putative EU law rights, that procedural rule must be set aside — national procedural rules cannot prevent adequate interim protection of EU law rights
options.C: That interim relief pending a preliminary reference must always be granted where the applicant shows a prima facie case
options.D: That the House of Lords has inherent jurisdiction to suspend Acts of Parliament

questions.explanation: Factortame I is the landmark ruling on interim relief: the CJEU held that a national procedural rule preventing a court from granting interim injunctions against the Crown must be set aside. If EU law rights require interim protection and national law denies the court the power to grant it, EU law requires the national court to assume that power. Supremacy and effectiveness of EU law require national courts to protect EU rights with all available remedies, including interim relief — even if national law as such does not provide for the specific remedy.

questions.question: A preliminary ruling on validity that finds an EU act to be invalid — what are the consequences?

options.A: The act is automatically removed from the Official Journal
options.B: The ruling is binding on the referring court in the main case and has the same erga omnes effect as an Article 263 annulment — the institution whose act was declared invalid must take necessary measures to comply; the act is effectively inapplicable throughout the EU
options.C: The act continues in force but the referring member state is granted an exemption
options.D: A validity ruling merely declares the act invalid in the specific case — other member states must conduct their own validity assessments

questions.explanation: A CJEU ruling declaring an EU act invalid under Article 267 has the same practical effect as an Article 263 annulment — it makes the act inapplicable throughout the EU (erga omnes). The CJEU has confirmed that once validity is declared, it would be inconsistent to treat the act as still applicable in other member states. The institution must take remedial action (adopt a new, valid act or withdraw the invalid one). The temporal effects may be limited (as with Article 264(2)) to protect legal certainty.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the preliminary reference procedure is CORRECT regarding the relationship between the referring court and the CJEU?

options.A: The CJEU acts as an appellate court — it may modify the national court's findings of fact
options.B: The procedure is cooperative, not appellate — the CJEU answers the EU law question and returns the case to the national court, which then applies the EU law answer to the facts of the case to give the final judgment
options.C: The CJEU determines the outcome of the national case after receiving the preliminary reference
options.D: The national court must follow the exact wording of the CJEU's answer in drafting its judgment

questions.explanation: The preliminary reference procedure is a cooperative dialogue between courts, not an appellate hierarchy. The CJEU's role is limited to answering the EU law question referred — it does not decide the main case. The national court retains jurisdiction over the factual findings, national law issues, and the ultimate judgment in the case. The national court receives the CJEU's EU law ruling and then applies it to the specific facts and national law to reach its decision. This division of labour is fundamental to the procedure's design.

questions.question: What is the difference between a preliminary ruling on interpretation and one on validity?

options.A: There is no legal difference — both have the same effect
options.B: Interpretation rulings clarify the meaning of EU law (national courts may give their own interpretation but should refer where in doubt); validity rulings assess whether EU secondary legislation breaches higher EU law norms — only the CJEU can declare EU acts invalid (national courts may not)
options.C: Validity rulings apply only to Directives; interpretation rulings apply only to Regulations
options.D: Interpretation rulings are binding; validity rulings are advisory only

questions.explanation: The distinction matters enormously: national courts have power to interpret EU law (even if they may refer for guidance), but they have no power to declare EU secondary legislation invalid — that power is reserved exclusively for the CJEU (Foto-Frost). Where a national court doubts the validity of an EU act, it must refer (it cannot itself declare it invalid, though it may grant limited interim relief pending the CJEU ruling). Both interpretation and validity rulings bind the referring court and, in practice, all other EU courts.

questions.question: In Interedil Srl v Fallimento Interedil Srl [2011] (C-396/09), the CJEU confirmed which principle about the relationship between its preliminary rulings and subsequent national court decisions?

options.A: National courts may depart from a preliminary ruling if they provide detailed reasons
options.B: The referring court is bound by the CJEU's preliminary ruling in deciding the main case — it must apply the CJEU's interpretation; it cannot disregard it or decide to its opposite without making a new reference
options.C: The CJEU's ruling is binding only on the judge who made the reference, not on the court as an institution
options.D: National courts may apply their own 'settled national case law' in preference to a CJEU preliminary ruling

questions.explanation: The referring court is bound by the CJEU's preliminary ruling — it must apply the CJEU's interpretation of EU law to the facts of the case. It cannot substitute its own view or ignore the ruling. If the national court disagrees with the ruling after further reflection or if circumstances change, it must either apply the ruling or make a new reference. It cannot simply decide contrary to the CJEU's answer. This binding effect ensures the cooperative mechanism actually produces uniform application of EU law in the specific case.
